title: On the dignity of disagreements that don't resolve
summary: Argue that unresolved disagreements can be a mark of intellectual health rather than failure — and that the compulsion to reach consensus too quickly often sacrifices truth for comfort.
topic: The value of disagreements that persist without resolution
prompt: Write a 600–800-word argumentative essay defending the value of disagreements that remain unresolved. Argue that some disagreements reflect genuine differences in values, evidence, or experience that cannot be collapsed without dishonesty, and that the social pressure toward premature consensus is itself a form of intellectual harm. Use inversion at least once, a cleft sentence, and a 'granted… even so…' concession. Avoid the language of conflict resolution and debate coaching. Treat disagreement as a philosophical and ethical question, not a communication problem.
hintStarters: Only rarely does the persistence of a disagreement signal failure; more often it signals that something real is at stake…
What the compulsion to resolve disagreement misses is not the value of consensus but…
Granted, unresolved disagreements are uncomfortable and can harden into mutual incomprehension. Even so,…

hintVocab.meaningVi: bất đồng không thể giải quyết
chủ nghĩa đa nguyên về giá trị
sức căng có tính xây dựng
nhu cầu về sự kết thúc, giải quyết
những giá trị không thể so sánh
thúc đẩy hoà bình, tránh xung đột
giữ bất đồng ở trạng thái mở

sampleText:
Only rarely does the persistence of a disagreement signal failure; more often it signals that something real is at stake — something that neither party can reasonably abandon without misrepresenting their own understanding. A debate about whether a government should prioritise economic growth or ecological restraint does not end in a handshake because neither position is simply an error; both reflect commitments that draw on real values and real evidence. To resolve that disagreement by formula — to paper over it with a declaration of shared goals — is not a political achievement. It is a form of intellectual evasion.

The pressure to reach consensus is understandable and not always wrong. In practical decisions — where something must be done, and where delay costs lives or resources — the ability to find workable agreement is a genuine virtue. But the demand for closure has a way of migrating from practical necessity into intellectual culture, where it does not belong. In seminars, in editorial meetings, in diplomatic back-rooms, in long marriages, there operates a tacit norm that disagreement is temporary, a friction on the way to a final agreement where both parties have moved. The person who refuses to move — who holds the disagreement open because they genuinely believe it is not resolvable — is cast as obstinate rather than honest.

What the compulsion to resolve disagreement misses is not the value of consensus but the category of reasonable disagreement: the space in which two thoughtful, well-informed people, attending to the same evidence, reach different conclusions because they are weighing different values or working from different but equally legitimate frameworks. Isaiah Berlin called this value pluralism — the philosophical recognition that some goods genuinely compete and that no framework can maximise all of them simultaneously. On such questions, forced conciliation is not synthesis; it is the subordination of one set of values to another under the guise of agreement. The result is not consensus; it is capitulation by the weaker party.

It is the dishonesty of false resolution, not the discomfort of continued disagreement, that most deserves our concern. When two parties reach a position neither fully endorses — a formula to which both can assent under pressure without either having genuinely moved — they have not resolved their disagreement; they have obscured it. The original contested terrain is still there beneath the diplomatic language, waiting for the next stress. Genuine convergence, where it occurs, is recognisable precisely because it is unsolicited: one party finds themselves changed by the encounter, not merely worn down by it.

Granted, unresolved disagreements are uncomfortable and can harden into mutual incomprehension. Even so, an honest impasse is preferable to a dishonest agreement, for the same reason that a known diagnosis is preferable to a misdiagnosis offered for comfort. The person who tells you that your situation is uncertain and your options are all imperfect is doing you more good than the person who tells you that everything will resolve satisfactorily if you follow a procedure that will not actually work. The same is true of intellectual life. A disagreement held open with care and rigour is more epistemically useful than one collapsed by social pressure into a resolution that satisfies neither party's actual position.

There is also a relational dignity in the disagreement that persists. When two people continue to disagree — to argue carefully, to revisit the evidence, to acknowledge what remains genuinely contested — they are treating each other as interlocutors rather than problems to be managed. The demand that a disagreement be resolved is, in some cases, a demand that the other person become simpler. To resist that demand, to hold the complexity of a real difference open, is not a failure of communication. It is a form of respect — for the difficulty of the question, for the standpoint of the other person, and for the intellectual integrity that genuine inquiry requires.

Not every disagreement is worth sustaining, and not every impasse reflects genuine incommensurability; some are products of bad faith, poor evidence, or inattention. But the remedy for those is better argument, not premature consensus. The disagreement that ends in honest acknowledgement that it will not resolve is not a conversation that failed. It is one that went as far as the truth allowed.

sampleGrammarNotes.title: Inversion after 'Only rarely' for measured argumentative authority
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyVi: Câu 'Only rarely does the persistence of a disagreement signal failure' đảo trợ động từ 'does' trước chủ ngữ 'the persistence of a disagreement' sau cụm phủ định 'Only rarely'. Đây là đảo ngữ trang trọng không chỉ nhấn mạnh tính ngoại lệ ('chỉ hiếm khi') mà còn đặt ngay luận điểm trung tâm của bài — rằng sự dai dẳng không phải là thất bại.
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyEn: 'Only rarely does the persistence of a disagreement signal failure' inverts 'does' before the subject after 'Only rarely' — a formal C1 inversion. The 'only' intensifies the negative fronting, making the claim more precise and measured: not 'never' but 'only rarely'. The inversion itself performs intellectual care.

sampleGrammarNotes.title: Cleft sentence to isolate the real object of concern
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyVi: Câu 'It is the dishonesty of false resolution, not the discomfort of continued disagreement, that most deserves our concern' dùng cấu trúc cleft 'It is X, not Y, that Z' để đặt trọng tâm vào sự thiếu trung thực của giải pháp giả tạo — đồng thời bác bỏ sự khó chịu của bất đồng là lý do thực sự để lo ngại. Đây là động thái chiến lược của bài: đảo ngược kỳ vọng thông thường.
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyEn: 'It is the dishonesty of false resolution, not the discomfort of continued disagreement, that most deserves our concern' uses 'It is X, not Y, that Z' to redirect concern from the intuitive target (discomfort) to the real problem (dishonesty) — a reversal that enacts the essay's central argument syntactically.

sampleTranslationVi:
Chỉ hiếm khi sự tồn tại dai dẳng của một bất đồng báo hiệu thất bại; thường hơn, nó báo hiệu rằng có điều gì đó thực sự đang bị đe doạ — điều gì đó mà không bên nào có thể hợp lý từ bỏ mà không xuyên tạc hiểu biết của chính họ. Một cuộc tranh luận về việc chính phủ có nên ưu tiên tăng trưởng kinh tế hay kiềm chế sinh thái không kết thúc bằng cái bắt tay vì không vị trí nào đơn giản là sai lầm. Che đậy nó bằng một tuyên bố về mục tiêu chung không phải là thành tựu chính trị. Đó là một hình thức né tránh trí tuệ.

Những gì sự thôi thúc giải quyết bất đồng bỏ lỡ không phải là giá trị của sự đồng thuận mà là phạm trù của bất đồng hợp lý. Trên những câu hỏi như vậy, sự hoà giải cưỡng bức không phải là tổng hợp; đó là sự phục tùng của một tập hợp giá trị với tập hợp khác dưới vỏ bọc của sự đồng thuận. Kết quả không phải là sự đồng thuận; đó là sự đầu hàng của bên yếu hơn.

Chính sự thiếu trung thực của giải pháp giả tạo, không phải sự khó chịu của bất đồng tiếp diễn, mới đáng được quan tâm nhất. Sự hội tụ thực sự, khi xảy ra, có thể nhận ra chính xác vì nó không được yêu cầu: một bên nhận thấy mình đã thay đổi bởi cuộc gặp, không chỉ bị mòn đi bởi nó.

Cứ cho là những bất đồng chưa được giải quyết là khó chịu và có thể trở nên cứng nhắc thành sự không hiểu nhau lẫn nhau. Dù vậy, một bế tắc trung thực được ưa thích hơn một thỏa thuận không trung thực, vì lý do tương tự mà một chẩn đoán đã biết được ưa thích hơn chẩn đoán sai được đưa ra để an ủi.

Cũng có một phẩm giá quan hệ trong bất đồng tồn tại. Khi hai người tiếp tục bất đồng — tranh luận cẩn thận, xem xét lại bằng chứng, thừa nhận những gì vẫn còn thực sự tranh chấp — họ đang đối xử với nhau như những người đối thoại hơn là những vấn đề cần được quản lý. Bất đồng kết thúc trong sự thừa nhận trung thực rằng nó sẽ không giải quyết không phải là cuộc trò chuyện thất bại. Đó là cuộc trò chuyện đã đi xa đến mức sự thật cho phép.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence correctly applies inversion after 'Only rarely'?
options: Only rarely the persistence of a disagreement signals failure.
Only rarely does the persistence of a disagreement signal failure.
Only rarely, disagreements signal failure when they persist.
The persistence of a disagreement only rarely signals failure.
mcQuestions.explanation: After 'Only rarely', the auxiliary 'does' must precede the subject 'the persistence of a disagreement' — obligatory inversion after a negative adverb.
mcQuestions.hint: Negative fronting with 'Only rarely' requires subject-auxiliary inversion.

mcQuestions.prompt: What does the essay say about the persistence of a disagreement?
options: It always signals that one party is being obstinate
It rarely signals failure; more often it signals that something genuinely real and important is at stake
It signals that both parties need better communication training
It is a sign that the disagreement should be referred to a mediator
mcQuestions.explanation: The opening line states: 'Only rarely does the persistence of a disagreement signal failure; more often it signals that something real is at stake.'
mcQuestions.hint: Look at the essay's first sentence.

mcQuestions.prompt: What is 'value pluralism' as described by the essay?
options: The belief that all cultural values are equally valid
The philosophical recognition that some genuine goods compete and that no framework can maximise all of them simultaneously
A political system that accommodates multiple parties
The practice of teaching children several value systems
mcQuestions.explanation: The essay attributes to Isaiah Berlin the concept of 'value pluralism — the philosophical recognition that some goods genuinely compete and that no framework can maximise all of them simultaneously.'
mcQuestions.hint: Look for the Berlin reference and its definition.

mcQuestions.prompt: What does 'forced conciliation' produce, according to the essay?
options: A durable peace that both parties can build on
The subordination of one party's values to another's, disguised as agreement
A creative synthesis that transcends both original positions
Reduced hostility between parties
mcQuestions.explanation: The essay states: 'forced conciliation is not synthesis; it is the subordination of one set of values to another under the guise of agreement.'
mcQuestions.hint: Look for 'forced conciliation is not synthesis'.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses a cleft to identify the real problem?
options: The discomfort of continued disagreement, not false resolution's dishonesty, deserves concern.
It is the dishonesty of false resolution, not the discomfort of continued disagreement, that most deserves our concern.
Both false resolution and continued disagreement deserve equal concern.
What deserves our concern is the discomfort that disagreements cause.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'It is X, not Y, that Z' foregrounds dishonesty and redirects concern from discomfort — a C1 cleft that enacts the essay's central reversal.
mcQuestions.hint: Look for the 'It is … not … that …' structure.

mcQuestions.prompt: How does the essay describe genuine convergence?
options: As the result of skilled mediation and formal negotiation
As recognisable precisely because it is unsolicited — one party is changed by the encounter, not worn down by it
As the moment when both parties agree on a compromise formula
As an outcome that can be manufactured through patient dialogue
mcQuestions.explanation: The essay says: 'Genuine convergence, where it occurs, is recognisable precisely because it is unsolicited: one party finds themselves changed by the encounter, not merely worn down by it.'
mcQuestions.hint: Look for 'unsolicited' in the paragraph about false resolution.

options: Even so, unresolved disagreements are uncomfortable. Granted, an honest impasse is preferable.
Granted, unresolved disagreements are uncomfortable and can harden into mutual incomprehension. Even so, an honest impasse is preferable to a dishonest agreement.
Even so and granted — disagreements are both useful and uncomfortable.
Granted, mutual incomprehension is bad. Even so, dishonest agreement is worse — this order is optional.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Granted' concedes that unresolved disagreements are uncomfortable; 'Even so' pivots to the preference for honest impasse over dishonest agreement.
mcQuestions.hint: The concession always comes before the counter-argument.

mcQuestions.prompt: What analogy does the essay use to explain why honest impasse is preferable to dishonest agreement?
options: A broken machine versus a working one
A known diagnosis versus a false reassurance offered for comfort
A map versus a photograph
An unfinished building versus a condemned one
mcQuestions.explanation: The essay says an honest impasse is preferable to a dishonest agreement 'for the same reason that a known diagnosis is preferable to a misdiagnosis offered for comfort.'
mcQuestions.hint: Look for the medical analogy in paragraph five.

mcQuestions.prompt: What does the essay mean by 'the demand that a disagreement be resolved is…a demand that the other person become simpler'?
options: Resolving disagreements requires both parties to simplify their vocabulary
Pressuring someone to give up their position forces them to deny the complexity of their actual view
Simple people are easier to disagree with
Simplicity is a virtue that enables resolution
mcQuestions.explanation: The essay argues that holding the disagreement open is 'a form of respect' for the complexity of the other person's standpoint — forcing resolution denies that complexity.
mcQuestions.hint: Look for the sentence about what it means to 'resist that demand'.

mcQuestions.prompt: What does 'capitulation by the weaker party' describe?
options: A military surrender following defeat in battle
The forced agreement of the party with less social or institutional power when genuine disagreement is not permitted to stand
The natural tendency of less experienced debaters to concede
The legal obligation to accept a court's judgement
mcQuestions.explanation: The essay uses 'capitulation' to describe what happens when forced conciliation produces an outcome that is not genuine synthesis but the submission of the weaker party.
mcQuestions.hint: Look at the sentence that ends '…it is the subordination of one set of values to another.'

mcQuestions.prompt: What does 'the demand for closure has a way of migrating from practical necessity into intellectual culture' mean?
options: Debates in intellectual settings are more likely to be unresolved than practical ones
A norm appropriate for urgent practical decisions wrongly spreads into intellectual contexts where truth matters more than speed
Intellectual culture is more resistant to closure than practical fields
Closure is a concept that originated in therapy and spread to other fields
mcQuestions.explanation: The essay argues closure is appropriate in practical decisions (where something must be done) but harmful when imported into intellectual culture, where premature resolution distorts truth.
mcQuestions.hint: Look for the contrast between 'practical decisions' and 'intellectual culture'.

mcQuestions.prompt: What does 'epistemically useful' mean in the context of the essay?
options: Useful for improving memory
Useful for generating or preserving genuine knowledge and honest understanding
Useful for passing academic examinations
Useful for improving classroom performance
mcQuestions.explanation: The essay describes a disagreement held open with care as 'more epistemically useful than one collapsed by social pressure into a resolution that satisfies neither party's actual position.' Epistemic = relating to knowledge.
mcQuestions.hint: Think about what 'epistemic' relates to — knowledge and how it is justified.

options: Values that cannot be translated across cultural boundaries
Values that genuinely compete and cannot both be maximised by any single framework
Values that are too small to be measured
Values that all reasonable people share equally
mcQuestions.explanation: The essay uses value pluralism — of which incommensurability is a key element — to describe the recognition that some goods genuinely compete and no framework can maximise all simultaneously.
mcQuestions.hint: Think about what 'incommensurable' means — not measurable by the same standard.

mcQuestions.prompt: How does the essay frame the relational value of sustained disagreement?
options: As a sign of healthy competition between intellectually equal parties
As a form of respect — treating the other person as an interlocutor whose complexity deserves acknowledgement
As evidence of good debating skills
As a way of testing the strength of a relationship
mcQuestions.explanation: The essay says continuing to disagree carefully means 'treating each other as interlocutors rather than problems to be managed' — a form of respect for complexity.
mcQuestions.hint: Look for the paragraph beginning 'There is also a relational dignity…'

mcQuestions.prompt: What does the essay's final sentence mean?
options: The best conversations always end in agreement
A conversation that ends in honest acknowledged impasse is not a failure — it reached the limit of what the truth permitted
Truth is the most important goal in any conversation
Conversations that do not resolve are incomplete and should be continued
mcQuestions.explanation: The final line — 'It is one that went as far as the truth allowed' — frames honest impasse as a form of success: having followed the inquiry as far as it could honestly go.
mcQuestions.hint: Look at how the essay defines success for a disagreement.

criticalThinkingQuestion: Think of a disagreement in your own life — with a person, or about an issue — that has never fully resolved. Is that because no one tried hard enough, or because the disagreement reflects a genuine difference in values or experience? Using the essay's framework, how would you evaluate whether the disagreement should be held open or pursued toward resolution?
